findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and update BOTH `plan_review_decide` invocations in `gates/plan-review.yaml` (code_grounded branch AND non-agentic else branch) to forward `review_phase` from `steps.precheck.outputs.review_phase`.
evidence: plan-review.yaml lines ~167-175: decide step in code_grounded branch uses plan_review_decide with findings/verifications/det_blocking/det_advisory only
plan-review.yaml lines ~247-255: a second decide step in the non-agentic else branch also uses plan_review_decide with the same four inputs and no review_phase
Plan says only 'gates/plan-review.yaml maps it into the decide step' — singular, no mention of both branches
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'gates/plan-review.yaml maps it [review_phase] into the decide step' as if there is a single plan_review_decide invocation in the YAML, but reading the actual plan-review.yaml shows TWO separate plan_review_decide invocations: one inside the code_grounded branch (step id 'decide', lines ~167-175) and one in the non-agentic else branch (step id 'decide', lines ~247-255). Updating only one of the two YAML wiring sites would leave the non-agentic branch passing plan_review_decide without review_phase, silently dropping execution-floor enforcement for all non-code-grounded reviews.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Thread phase through the production workflow: ... llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml maps it into the decide step'
scenarios: Non-agentic verifier path (no code-grounded findings) skips the execution floor because the decide step never receives review_phase
Silent regression: execution-phase reviews with only non-code-grounded criteria pass sub-0.80 findings that should be blocked
findings.suggested_fix: In plan-review.yaml, add `review_phase: ${{ steps.precheck.outputs.review_phase }}` to the `with:` block of every `plan_review_decide` step — currently at least two call sites exist (the code_grounded branch decide and the else-branch decide).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact YAML additions needed in `gates/plan-review.yaml` to thread `review_phase` from `steps.precheck.outputs.review_phase` into both `plan_review_decide` `with:` blocks (in both verify_branch arms), and declare `review_phase` in `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json`'s `properties`.
evidence: plan-review.yaml verified: both plan_review_decide call-sites have `with:` blocks containing only `findings`, `verifications`, `det_blocking`, `det_advisory` — no `review_phase` key
plan-review.yaml verified: workflow-level `inputs:` section (lines 18-29) contains only `ticket_id` and `probe_criteria`

plan text: 'workflow_ops.plan_review_precheck emits the compiled value, llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml maps it into the decide step, and plan_review_decide passes execution_review=(review_phase == "execution")'
plan_review_decide_input.schema.json verified: `review_phase` is absent from `properties`; additionalProperties: true so it would pass through, but it is not declared
findings.finding: The `review_phase` threading from `plan_review_precheck` outputs into `plan_review_decide` is specified in prose but has no co-located mechanism in `gates/plan-review.yaml`. Both decide call-sites in the verified YAML have no `review_phase:` key in their `with:` blocks, and the workflow-level `inputs:` section (verified: only `ticket_id` and `probe_criteria`) has no `review_phase` input. The plan says 'gates/plan-review.yaml maps it into the decide step' but does not state which exact YAML lines to add, nor does it add `review_phase` to the `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json` (which currently has `additionalProperties: true` but omits the field from `properties`).
findings.location: Implementation § 'Thread phase through the production workflow'; src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml lines 167-176 and 247-256 (both decide with-blocks); src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_decide_input.schema.json
scenarios: An executor adding `review_phase` to precheck output but not updating the YAML with-blocks produces a decide step that never receives it — execution reviews silently run at planning thresholds

The AC 'A production workflow integration test starts from compiled execution phase, threads review_phase through precheck/YAML/decide' would fail to catch this because the threading in YAML is never specified
findings.suggested_fix: Add `review_phase: ${{ steps.precheck.outputs.review_phase }}` to both `plan_review_decide` `with:` blocks in plan-review.yaml (lines ~172-176 and ~252-256). Add `review_phase` to `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json` `properties` as `{ "type": "string", "enum": ["planning", "execution"] }` and add it to `required`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the `execution_review` parameter threading rule with the `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` call-site inventory: explicitly name every call site (workflow_ops.py line 484, gate_dispatch.py line 375) and state whether each passes `execution_review=True` or `False`.
evidence: orchestrator.py verified: `pass3_over_findings(findings, verifs)` — no `execution_review` parameter exists
orchestrator.py verified: `_threshold_for` closure at line 694 is `def _threshold_for(criteria: Any) -> tuple[float, bool]: return _criteria.threshold_for(criteria, crit_by_id, gate='plan_review')`
plan text: 'In `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings(findings, verifs, *, execution_review: bool = False)`, the local `_threshold_for` closure captures that parameter'
workflow_ops.py line 484: `decided = [*too_big, *shed, *orchestrator.pass3_over_findings(rest, rest_verifs)]` — the call site would also need updating
findings.finding: The modification protocol for the local `_threshold_for` closure inside `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` is stated in the Pass-3 section but not co-located with the closure's definition. The plan says 'the local `_threshold_for` closure captures that parameter, calls `_criteria.threshold_for(...)`, and destructures `(block_threshold, blocking_enabled)`. When `execution_review and blocking_enabled`, it returns `(max(block_threshold, 0.80), blocking_enabled)`.' However, `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` (verified at lines 678-703) is a module-level function with a fixed `(findings, verifs)` signature, and its `_threshold_for` closure (line 694-695) currently delegates to `_criteria.threshold_for` with no extra parameters. The plan does not specify whether `execution_review` is a new keyword parameter on `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` itself or is captured from the outer `plan_review_decide` scope.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Thread phase through the production workflow' (Pass-3 paragraph); src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py lines 678-703
scenarios: An executor modifying orchestrator.pass3_over_findings to accept execution_review but forgetting to update the workflow_ops.py call site (line 484) causes the parameter to always default to False, silently bypassing execution-floor enforcement
The gate_dispatch.py call at line 375 also calls orchestrator.pass3_over_findings — the plan does not specify whether this site also receives execution_review

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground `PlanReviewGeneration.ticket_store_revision` against a specific existing `_store` primitive (e.g. `event_append` HEAD-resolution or a new `current_head(tracker)` helper), justify why the existing write lock does not already serialize phase/material/generation integrity, and document how the outer 3-retry loop composes with the inner `_with_index_lock_retry` / `_with_transient_head_retry` / `lock.acquire` retries to avoid retry-loop interaction.
evidence: Plan: 'Retry outside the lock at most three times only for an unstable tracker revision: read clean tracker HEAD, collect a fresh generation, read HEAD again; unequal HEADs emit structured WARN plan_review_generation_retry with attempt/before/after and retry.'

Existing code: `_with_transient_head_retry` (gitutil.py) already retries up to 3× on 'could not parse HEAD'; `lock.acquire` uses 2 attempts with 30s timeout; `event_append` has its own index.lock retry loop.
The plan's 'clean tracker HEAD' / 'ticket_store_revision' term is never mapped to an existing primitive (no `git rev-parse HEAD` call site exists in the plan_review module today).

ABSENCE: no justification is given for why the existing write lock (which already serializes all appends) does not already protect the phase/material/relationship atomicity the generation check aims to provide.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel `PlanReviewGeneration` pre-sign transaction with an outside-the-lock HEAD-equality retry loop (read HEAD → collect generation → read HEAD → compare; retry up to 3× on instability) without establishing why the existing write-lock + `event_append` retry infrastructure is insufficient or how this outer loop composes safely with the inner retries already present in `_with_index_lock_retry` / `_with_transient_head_retry` and the 2-attempt `lock.acquire` in compact.py.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction' and 'Before signing, retain one immutable initial_generation... Retry outside the lock at most three times only for an unstable tracker revision'
scenarios: The outer 3-retry HEAD-instability loop and the inner lock-acquire/index-lock retries both fire on the same git transient, consuming retries at two levels and potentially masking genuine stale-generation failures as transient HEAD issues.

If 'clean tracker HEAD' means `git rev-parse HEAD`, calling it before lock acquisition creates a read-then-lock window where the HEAD can change before the lock is acquired — the check would be spuriously stable or spuriously unstable depending on concurrent agent activity.
A reader unfamiliar with the existing `_store` retry infrastructure would duplicate its behavior rather than reuse it, creating a maintenance burden.

findings.finding: The plan says 'workflow_ops passes the compiled review phase into this parameter' (the new execution_review keyword arg on orchestrator.pass3_over_findings), but plan_review_decide in workflow_ops.py currently only receives findings/verifications/det_blocking/det_advisory from its StepContext inputs — it has no access to the compiled plan_review_phase field. The plan does not specify whether (a) plan_review_phase is threaded as a new workflow step input from plan_review_precheck, (b) plan_review_decide reads compiled state via _reads.show_ticket, or (c) some other mechanism. Without specifying this, workflow_ops.py's required edit is underspecified.
findings.location: Implementation section, Pass-3 bullet: 'workflow_ops passes the compiled review phase into this parameter'; workflow_ops.py::plan_review_decide (line 484)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the module location and class hierarchy for `ManifestFormatError` (e.g., `class ManifestFormatError(ValueError)` in `manifest.py`) and `SidecarReviewPhaseError` (e.g., `class SidecarReviewPhaseError(ValueError)` in `sidecar.py`) co-located with their respective parsers.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/manifest.py verified: no `ManifestFormatError` class defined anywhere in the file
search for `ManifestFormatError` in src/: no matches
search for `SidecarReviewPhaseError` in src/: no matches
plan text: 'The helper raises `ManifestFormatError` for duplicates signaled by callers...' and 'translates `ManifestFormatError` to `SidecarReviewPhaseError`'
findings.finding: `ManifestFormatError` and `SidecarReviewPhaseError` are referenced as exception types that callers and parsers raise, but neither exists in the codebase (verified: no `ManifestFormatError` in manifest.py or anywhere in src/) and neither has its module location, class hierarchy, or fields defined in the plan.
findings.location: Implementation § manifest helper ('The helper raises `ManifestFormatError`...'); § sidecar helper ('translates `ManifestFormatError` to `SidecarReviewPhaseError`'); Acceptance Criteria 'Every named phase/floor syntax error raises `ManifestFormatError`'
scenarios: An executor creating ManifestFormatError as a subclass of ValueError vs. a standalone class changes whether existing bare `except Exception` guards in manifest parsers catch it — the hierarchy is load-bearing for the error-containment contract
SidecarReviewPhaseError's module location (manifest.py? sidecar.py? a new exceptions.py?) determines import paths for every consumer that catches it
findings.suggested_fix: Add `class ManifestFormatError(ValueError): pass` near the top of `manifest.py` and `class SidecarReviewPhaseError(ValueError): pass` near the top of `sidecar.py`; the plan's translation step then catches `ManifestFormatError` and re-raises `SidecarReviewPhaseError`.

evidence: 34 acceptance-criteria items (> 25)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 8 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a feature flag or staged rollout mechanism (e.g. opt-in per repo/org, or a percentage canary) so the new phase projection and execution-floor enforcement can be enabled incrementally rather than all-at-once via the cache-version bump.
evidence: 'Bump reducer/_cache.py::_REDUCER_CACHE_VERSION so the first post-upgrade reduction applies this once'
No mention of a feature flag, percentage rollout, or canary anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: No feature flag, canary, or staged rollout mechanism is specified. The `_REDUCER_CACHE_VERSION` bump activates the new `plan_review_phase` projection for all tickets on first read after upgrade — a one-shot all-traffic flip with no gradual rollout path.
findings.location: Implementation § reducer bootstrap / Rollout section absent
scenarios: A buggy bootstrap rule mis-categorizes all open tickets as execution-phase, immediately blocking all plan-review claims until hotfix is deployed.
The floor enforcement activates for 100% of reviews simultaneously, causing unexpected blocking at scale.
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a config flag `plan_review_phase_enabled: bool` (default False, set True per deployment tier). The bootstrap and floor enforcement code gates on this flag; the cache-version bump only activates bootstrap logic when the flag is on. This allows canary validation before full rollout.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the conditional rollback description with a single committed post-rollback state assertion (e.g., 'after rollback completes, `plan_review_phase` is absent from all compiled states; all enforcement treats absence as planning-legacy until forward upgrade reboots it'), and update the rollback AC to verify this invariant.
evidence: Two divergent post-rollback states are described with no criterion for which applies ('preserve' vs 'absent')
T11 criterion: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'
AC line: 'The rollback test proves old-code raw-event re-reduction drops the unknown field and forward upgrade reboots it from compiled status' — the test covers only one of the two scenarios
findings.finding: The rollback description is conditional rather than a committed post-rollback state assertion: the plan says old reducers 'preserve the unknown snapshot field during re-compaction' in one scenario but 'if old code fully re-reduces raw events after its own cache bump, the phase field is absent' in another, without specifying which state the system will be in after a rollback completes. This ambiguity means operators cannot verify correct post-rollback behavior.
findings.location: Implementation — Rollback paragraph: 'old reducers preserve the unknown snapshot field during re-compaction. If old code fully re-reduces raw events after its own cache bump, the phase field is absent'
scenarios: An operator rolls back, some tickets have only snapshots (old reducer preserves the field), others have been fully re-reduced (field is absent) — the system is in a mixed state with no documented invariant

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define one canonical module-level constant (e.g. `EXECUTION_FLOOR = 0.80`) imported by manifest generation, orchestrator pass3, sidecar schema validation, and compatibility checks, so any protocol floor change is a single-point edit.
evidence: manifest line: `priority-floor: 0.80`
orchestrator: `max(block_threshold, 0.80)`
sidecar schema: `priority_floor: 0.8` (JSON number)
plan states: 'Raising it later invalidates lower-floor execution attestations'
No single named constant or canonical module for the 0.80 value is defined across the plan
findings.finding: The literal `0.80` / `0.8` floor constant appears in at least three separated locations across components — manifest generation (`priority-floor: 0.80`), orchestrator logic (`max(block_threshold, 0.80)`), and the sidecar schema (`priority_floor: 0.8`) — with no single canonical definition. The plan acknowledges the floor can evolve ('raising it later invalidates lower-floor execution attestations') but provides no single authoritative source, increasing the risk of version drift between components when the protocol floor changes.
findings.location: Implementation sections: 'Restructure build_manifest...', 'pass3_over_findings...', 'Persist review-time phase data...'
scenarios: A future protocol change raises the floor to 0.85; the orchestrator is updated but the manifest generation retains 0.80, causing manifests to claim 0.80 while enforcement uses 0.85 — attestations signed under the old manifest value silently pass the wrong floor check.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `plan_review_constants.py` (or equivalent) exporting `EXECUTION_PRIORITY_FLOOR: float = 0.80`; import it in `build_manifest`, `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings`, and `sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata`; the AC for 'Execution manifests contain exactly `priority-floor: 0.80`' should assert the value equals the constant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the required deploy order for components that changed schemas (`workflow_ops` → `plan_review_decide` → `orchestrator`) and define backward-compatible defaults (e.g. absent `review_phase` treated as `planning`) so old and new versions can coexist safely during the rollout window.
evidence: 'add required review_phase: planning|execution to plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json and plan_review_decide_input.schema.json'
The plan marks the field as `required` in both schemas, meaning old workflow_ops would fail schema validation against new decide input schema.
No deploy ordering or backward-compatible default (e.g. defaulting absent `review_phase` to `planning`) is described for the transition window.
findings.finding: The deploy ordering between producers and consumers is not specified. `workflow_ops` emits the new `review_phase` field; `plan_review_decide` consumes it; `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` enforces the floor — but if old consumers read new manifests or old workflow_ops runs against new orchestrator code, the coexistence behavior is undefined.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Thread phase through the production workflow' / AC 'production workflow integration test'
scenarios: New orchestrator code deployed before new workflow_ops: decide step receives no `review_phase` field, fails schema validation, breaking all plan reviews mid-rollout.
Old orchestrator deployed with new manifests containing `review-phase:` and `priority-floor:` lines: old parsers that scan by prefix may silently ignore or choke on these new lines.
findings.suggested_fix: Make `review_phase` optional with a default of `planning` in both JSON schemas for the transition period; document that workflow_ops must be deployed before orchestrator, and that the field becomes required only after full rollout.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the Implementation section (or a dedicated Rationale subsection) explaining why the reducer-projection + manifest-recorded-phase approach is chosen: specifically, that claim-time status inference fails for tickets that transition open→in_progress mid-review (breaking the 'signature must not bind a phase that changed during review' goal), and that a separate PHASE event type would duplicate fork-resolution logic the STATUS projection already handles.
evidence: The entire Implementation section describes mechanism mechanics (what and how) but contains no sentence of the form 'This approach was chosen because …' or 'This is preferred over X because Y.'
The Rollback section mentions that 'old reducers preserve the unknown snapshot field during re-compaction' as a property, but not as a rationale for the reducer approach.
ABSENCE: no comparison to alternative design choices (e.g., claim-time status inference or a separate event type) that would justify the added reducer complexity.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the chosen approach as a full implementation specification but does not state a positive rationale — WHY the reducer-projection + manifest-recorded-phase mechanism was chosen over structurally different alternatives (e.g., inferring phase from current ticket status at claim time, or tracking phase via a dedicated PHASE event type). A reader implementing this plan cannot distinguish deliberate design choices from arbitrary ones, making future maintenance and rollback decisions harder.
findings.location: Implementation section (first bullet): 'Add reducer-maintained plan_review_phase: planning|execution to compiled state, distinct from prompt execution_mode.'
scenarios: A future maintainer considering whether to simplify by inferring phase from current status at claim time has no documented reason to preserve the reducer projection.
An operator planning a rollback sees 'documented loss of historical phase precision during the rollback window' without understanding why that tradeoff was accepted over a simpler design.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one paragraph before or after the first Implementation bullet, e.g.: 'This approach maintains phase in compiled state (derived from STATUS events) rather than inferring it at claim time, because a ticket may transition open→in_progress while the LLM review is in flight: claim-time inference would then mis-classify the already-signed planning attestation as an execution attestation, binding the wrong floor. Recording the signed phase in the manifest and comparing it to the current compiled phase is the only mechanism that correctly separates "phase the LLM ran under" from "phase at claim time."'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named rollback test file (e.g. `tests/unit/test_plan_review_phase_rollback.py`) to the Verification section with a concrete `pytest -q` command that covers: (a) old-reducer raw-event re-reduction drops the `plan_review_phase` field, (b) forward upgrade reboots it from compiled status, and (c) the precision-loss window behavior matches the documented guarantee.
evidence: The five named pytest invocations in Verification cover: test_plan_review_phase_reducer.py, test_plan_review_phase_manifest.py, test_plan_review_phase_validity.py, test_plan_review_execution_floor.py, test_plan_review_resign.py, test_plan_review_sign_transaction.py, tests/interfaces/lifecycle/*, test_plan_review_docs.py — none is a rollback test.
The final bullet says 'including the named integration and rollback tests' but no rollback test file is named anywhere in the plan.
The rollback AC requires testing old-code behavior (a different reducer version's compaction output), which needs a dedicated fixture and test file to be actionable.
findings.finding: The rollback acceptance criterion ('The rollback test proves old-code raw-event re-reduction drops the unknown field and forward upgrade reboots it from compiled status, with the documented precision-loss window') has no named test file or concrete proving command anywhere in the Verification section. The final Verification bullet references 'the named... rollback tests' but never actually names them, leaving the executor without a locatable, runnable artifact to satisfy this AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — rollback AC; Verification section — final bullet ('Run make check and the full non-LLM test suite, including the named integration and rollback tests')
scenarios: An executor implementing the feature has no target file for the rollback test, cannot verify the AC, and may omit the test entirely while still passing all named pytest commands.

A reviewer running `make check` and the named tests will see all green but the rollback AC will be untested, silently satisfying the completion criteria without proof of the documented rollback behavior.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the Verification section:
```
pytest -q tests/unit/test_plan_review_phase_rollback.py
```
and create that file with three test cases: (1) a fixture simulating a post-rollback snapshot (field absent) re-reduced by old logic produces no `plan_review_phase` key; (2) a forward upgrade from that snapshot bootstraps phase from compiled status; (3) an `open`/`idea` compiled status bootstraps `planning`, any other known status bootstraps `execution`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `PlanReviewGeneration.phase` for re-sign is set from the *sidecar's recorded phase* (not current compiled phase), so generation equality does not conflict with the phase compatibility table's cross-phase-compatible cases.
evidence: 'current execution + signed planning/legacy planning = compatible' (compatibility table)
'resign_plan_review calls this named parser, compares the result with current compiled phase, and refuses incompatible/malformed re-sign'
'PlanReviewGeneration is {phase, priority_floor, ...} where ... equality is dataclass equality across every field'
'A stable fresh generation unequal to the initial generation is terminal unsigned stale'

If sidecar.phase=planning and compiled.phase=execution, the fresh generation.phase=execution ≠ the initial_generation implied by the sidecar recording, creating a spurious stale failure despite valid compatibility.
findings.finding: Re-sign flow contradicts the generation-equality rule when the compatibility table permits mismatched phases. The compatibility table allows current=execution + signed/sidecar=planning as compatible, so `resign_plan_review` would pass the compatibility check and proceed. However, the generation-transaction rule requires the fresh generation (which captures current compiled phase=execution) to equal the initial_generation. If the sidecar recorded phase=planning and current compiled phase=execution, the generation's `phase` field differs, triggering 'terminal unsigned stale' — even though the compatibility table explicitly permits this combination.
findings.location: Implementation sections: 'Persist review-time phase data for failed-sign recovery' (re-sign) and 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction' (generation equality)
scenarios: A ticket moves from open→in_progress (now execution phase) after a planning-phase attestation was signed. Re-sign is attempted. Compatibility check passes (execution accepts planning). Generation is collected with phase=execution. Initial generation (from sidecar) had phase=planning. Generation equality fails → terminal stale. Re-sign is impossible despite the spec permitting it.
findings.suggested_fix: For re-sign, populate `PlanReviewGeneration.phase` from the sidecar's recorded review-time phase rather than the current compiled phase. This aligns the generation equality check with what was actually recorded, and cross-phase-compatible cases (execution accepts planning attestation) will correctly produce matching generations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that `PlanReviewGeneration` is a dataclass with exactly the fields `{phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, ticket_store_revision}` and that equality is field-wise across all five.
evidence: 'PlanReviewGeneration is {phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, ticket_store_revision} where relation_snapshot is child acaf\'s exact PlanRelationSnapshot and equality is dataclass equality across every field'
No AC line names PlanReviewGeneration's fields or verifies the struct shape.
The closest AC is 'Any stable phase, own-material, relationship, related-fingerprint, or generation-field change returns terminal unsigned stale' — which tests behavior but not struct definition.
findings.finding: `PlanReviewGeneration`'s struct field set `{phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, ticket_store_revision}` is described in the Implementation but has no acceptance criterion verifying its shape or completeness, leaving the boundary between this plan and child acaf's `PlanRelationSnapshot` under-specified at the AC level.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction'
scenarios: An implementer omits `priority_floor` from the dataclass (since phase already covers planning/execution) — behavior tests pass but the equality check misses floor drift, allowing a floor change to go undetected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `resign_plan_review` how a missing or unreadable sidecar file is handled (e.g., treat as legacy absent — planning/None — or return typed error), and add this case to the re-sign AC and test coverage in `test_plan_review_resign.py`.
evidence: 'Add `sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata(payload) -> ReviewPhaseMetadata`; it passes raw JSON values to `manifest.validate_review_phase_metadata` and translates `ManifestFormatError` to `SidecarReviewPhaseError`.'
'`resign_plan_review` calls this named parser, compares the result with current compiled phase, and refuses incompatible/malformed re-sign.'
No mention of what `resign_plan_review` does when the sidecar file is absent or the read itself raises an I/O exception
findings.finding: `sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata` is a new file-system I/O entry point introduced specifically for the re-sign path, but the plan specifies error handling only for key-shape/schema errors (translating `ManifestFormatError` to `SidecarReviewPhaseError`). It does not specify behavior when the sidecar file itself is missing or unreadable during `resign_plan_review` — a distinct failure mode from malformed content.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Persist review-time phase data for failed-sign recovery...' and 'resign_plan_review calls this named parser...'
scenarios: A sidecar file is deleted or corrupted between the original review and the re-sign attempt; `parse_review_phase_metadata` raises an unhandled I/O exception rather than returning a typed error, causing `resign_plan_review` to surface an unhandled traceback instead of a clean unsigned result.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback AC test and documentation entry proving that old-code manifest parsers tolerate (silently skip) the new `review-phase:` and `priority-floor:` lines, and that old sidecar parsers tolerate `plan_review_result_v2` extra keys without error.
evidence: 'Rollback disables claim/close enforcement first; old reducers preserve the unknown snapshot field during re-compaction.'

The plan asserts old manifest parsers use prefix scanning and so 'added lines do not change regver/material/dependency parsing' — but this covers forward parsing, not the rollback case where new-format attestations are read by old code.
No rollback AC tests for manifest or sidecar v2 backward compatibility with old parsers.
findings.finding: The rollback path does not address old-code parsers reading new manifests that contain the new `review-phase:` and `priority-floor:` lines. The plan only documents the reducer snapshot rollback (unknown field preserved/dropped); it does not specify whether old manifest parsers will silently ignore, error on, or misparse the new lines.
findings.location: Implementation § Rollback paragraph / 'Existing parsers scan by prefix rather than fixed offsets'
scenarios: After rollback to old code, existing signed attestations contain `review-phase:` lines; old parsers encounter an unrecognized prefix and either silently skip (safe) or raise an error (breaks claim/close for all previously-signed tickets).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the reducer cache bootstrap is idempotent and safe under concurrent access (e.g. the cache write is atomic/CAS, or the bootstrap is behind a per-ticket lock, or the cache store guarantees last-write-wins with a stable computed value), and that duplicate bootstrap log events are tolerated or prevented.
evidence: 'ordinary reads rebuild the local derived cache lazily and do not append ticket events'
No mention of locking, idempotency guard, or CAS on the cache write during bootstrap
The plan specifies the global write lock only for the signing step, not for cache initialization
findings.finding: The concurrent bootstrap race for `plan_review_phase` in the reducer cache is not addressed. Multiple concurrent readers of the same uninitialized ticket cache could each independently trigger bootstrap, potentially emitting multiple `plan_review_phase_bootstrap` INFO events or racing on the cache write.
findings.location: Implementation § reducer bootstrap / 'ordinary reads rebuild the local derived cache lazily'
scenarios: Two concurrent API requests for the same ticket both find an empty cache, both compute bootstrap phase, both write — last-write-wins is safe only if both compute the same value, but if the cache store is not atomic, a partial write could corrupt the cached state.
Multiple bootstrap INFO events emitted for the same ticket confuse observability tooling.
findings.suggested_fix: Since the bootstrap computation is deterministic (same raw events → same phase), document that concurrent bootstraps are safe because all writers compute the same value; add a dedup guard (e.g. check-then-write under a per-ticket lock or use atomic file rename) and suppress duplicate INFO events with a cache-hit check before logging.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the production-path integration test AC to explicitly require that `test_plan_review_sign_transaction.py` runs against a real (non-stubbed) ticket store — or document why a faithful test double is equivalent and name that rationale in the plan.
evidence: Plan names 'production signing and automatic execution-floor integrations' but does not constrain whether the tracker/ticket store is real or a test double.
The under-lock retry loop and atomic commit are the concurrency-safety centerpiece; a fake store that short-circuits locking never exercises this path.

Proxy-validation fires when the new defaulted/primary path is exercised only through a mock/offline substitute that bypasses the new behavior — no documented rationale explains why a substitute would be faithful.
findings.finding: The plan names `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_sign_transaction.py` and `test_plan_review_execution_floor.py` as 'production-path integration tests' covering 'under-lock HEAD re-verification, signature append, and commit end to end,' but never specifies that these run against a real (non-mocked) ticket store. If these tests use a test double that bypasses actual lock acquisition and atomic append, the suite goes green while the live signing path is unverified — a proxy-validation gap for the riskiest new behavior.
findings.location: Verification section / AC: 'The production-path integration test covers stable collection, under-lock HEAD re-verification, signature append, and commit end to end.'
scenarios: Integration tests use an in-memory or stub ticket store that never acquires a real lock; HEAD comparison and atomic commit are trivially satisfied; suite goes green; live concurrent signing is broken.
A future change to the lock acquisition path passes unit tests but the integration tests don't catch it because the store is mocked.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the relevant AC: 'The interface test uses the real ticket store (not a stub); lock acquisition, HEAD comparison, and atomic append are exercised against actual storage, not a test double. If an in-process substitute is used, document explicitly why it is lock-faithful.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `PlanReviewGeneration` has a second current call site justifying a named dataclass, or simplify to an inline dict / namedtuple used only within the sign-transaction module (YAGNI / Rule-of-Three).
evidence: Plan names exactly one consumer of PlanReviewGeneration: the sign transaction (full review, drift refresh, re-sign all 'collect it'). No second consumer is cited.
Grep for 'PlanReviewGeneration' in src/ returns no matches (not yet implemented), confirming the plan is the sole source of this name.

The plan's equality use case ('equality is dataclass equality across every field') is identically achieved with a dict, a namedtuple, or a simple tuple of the five fields — no dataclass-specific behaviour is used.
Rule-of-Three requires >=3 call sites for a reusable abstraction; 1 call site with no projected extension is below the bar.
findings.finding: `PlanReviewGeneration` is introduced as a named dataclass with only one described production call site (the sign transaction path), where dataclass equality over five fields is its sole distinguishing behaviour versus a plain dict or inline comparison — making it a premature abstraction by the Rule-of-Three criterion.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 8: 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction… `PlanReviewGeneration` is `{phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, ticket_store_revision}`'
scenarios: Implementer creates a formal dataclass + imports for a construct used in exactly one place, adding indirection with no benefit over an inline dict comparison.
Future additions may never materialise, leaving a one-consumer dataclass in perpetuity.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `PlanReviewGeneration` with a module-private `_GenerationSnapshot` NamedTuple (or just a plain dict with a helper function) local to the signing module. If the same struct is consumed by a second call site (e.g. the test helpers or the generation-changed error path), document that second site explicitly in the plan to satisfy Rule-of-Three.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state for v2 sidecars: specify whether old sidecar parsers tolerate unknown keys (additive/lenient) or reject them, and document the recovery path if old code encounters a v2 sidecar after rollback.
evidence: 'new full reviews always write JSON `review_phase`' — no description of what old code does when it reads these keys post-rollback
Rollback section addresses only the compiled-state snapshot field and reducer behavior; sidecar format rollback path is absent
T11 criterion: 'POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE: the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the concrete schema state of `plan_review_result_v2` sidecars after rollback: old code encountering new sidecars containing `review_phase` and `priority_floor` keys is never described. If old sidecar parsers are strict (reject unknown keys or treat them as malformed), a rollback leaves already-written v2 sidecars unreadable, causing failed-sign recovery to break silently or raise errors — a DATA LOSS RISK for in-flight reviews.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Persist review-time phase data for failed-sign recovery with an exact additive `plan_review_result_v2` schema' and Rollback section
scenarios: Operator rolls back the code after v2 sidecars have been written; old re-sign path reads a v2 sidecar and either crashes on unknown keys or misparses the phase as absent, silently treating an execution review as planning-legacy
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Rollback section: 'Old sidecar parsers must treat unknown top-level keys as ignored (lenient parse); v2-written sidecars remain readable by old code as legacy (both phase keys treated as absent). Confirm this in the sidecar parser contract and add a regression test that old-format parser round-trips a v2 payload without error.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either enumerate `PlanRelationSnapshot`'s fields and equality semantics in this plan, or explicitly state that this plan's generation transaction is contingent on child acaf's PlanRelationSnapshot being a frozen dataclass with value-equality semantics.
evidence: `PlanRelationSnapshot` search in src/: no matches — it does not yet exist (it is a child acaf deliverable)
plan text: '`PlanReviewGeneration` is `{phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, ticket_store_revision}` where `relation_snapshot` is child acaf's exact `PlanRelationSnapshot`'
plan text: 'equality is dataclass equality across every field'
The generation-changed terminal unsigned stale path ('A stable fresh generation unequal to the initial generation is terminal unsigned stale') depends on knowing when relation_snapshot changes
findings.finding: The `PlanReviewGeneration` type's `relation_snapshot` field is defined only as 'child acaf's exact `PlanRelationSnapshot`' with no enumeration of that type's fields in this plan. The generation equality rule is 'dataclass equality across every field' — but since `PlanRelationSnapshot`'s fields are not defined here, an executor cannot determine what constitutes a generation change for the relationship dimension, which is one of the five equality-checked fields.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction' (PlanReviewGeneration definition); Implementation § generation equality ('equality is dataclass equality across every field')
scenarios: If PlanRelationSnapshot is a mutable container (list of dicts vs. frozen dataclass), dataclass equality may not work as expected — an executor needs the field types to implement __eq__ correctly
Child acaf may define PlanRelationSnapshot with fields that are unhashable, making dataclass equality non-deterministic for the retry comparison

findings.finding: The rollback procedure described in `docs/plan-review-gate.md` is not covered by any test in the verification section. The plan specifies rollback behavior (disable enforcement, old reducers preserve unknown field, re-reduction from raw events drops the field) but no test exercises the rollback sequence or verifies old-reducer snapshot preservation.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Rollback' and Verification section

findings.finding: The `resign_plan_review` section refers to 'the shared phase helper' that validates grammar/policy of sidecar-recorded `review_phase`/`priority_floor` fields, but this helper is never named or given a module address in the plan. The manifest section introduces `manifest_review_phase()` and `manifest_priority_floor()` parsers, but those operate on manifest line lists — not sidecar JSON fields. The resign path reads sidecar JSON, not manifest lines, so it is unclear whether the 'shared phase helper' IS those two functions applied to a reconstructed line list, or a distinct helper, or something else entirely.
findings.location: Implementation §6: 'resign_plan_review treats absent legacy fields as planning, validates their grammar/policy through the shared phase helper, compares them with current compiled phase, and refuses incompatible/malformed re-sign.'

findings.finding: The sidecar `plan_review_result_v2` new fields `review_phase` and `priority_floor` are described as 'additive v2 fields' carried by the full LLM verdict sidecar, but their exact serialization contract (JSON field names, allowed string values for `review_phase`, whether `priority_floor` is serialized as a float or string, whether `None` vs. absent means different things) is never specified. The plan names them but provides no schema definition. This is critical because `resign_plan_review` must parse these exact field names and types from the raw JSON sidecar to drive re-sign decisions.
findings.location: Implementation §6: 'the full LLM verdict and plan_review_result_v2 sidecar carry review_phase and optional priority_floor from the pre-LLM generation; these are additive v2 fields'

findings.finding: The Implementation section specifies updating `docs/plan-review-gate.md` with the phase/floor contract, health compatibility table, fixed-floor rationale, generation transaction, and rollback behavior, but no AC verifies this artifact exists or is complete. The stated end-state includes documentation, but 'done' has no measurable check for it.
findings.location: Implementation — last bullet: 'Document the phase/floor manifest contract … in advanced docs/plan-review-gate.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`docs/plan-review-gate.md` exists and contains top-level sections for: phase/floor manifest contract, health compatibility table, fixed-floor rationale, generation transaction, and rollback behavior.' Evaluable by `grep` or `make docs-check` in the verification step.

findings.finding: The `docs/plan-review-gate.md` documentation deliverable (phase/floor manifest contract, health compatibility table, fixed-floor rationale, generation transaction, rollback behavior) is described in the Implementation section but has no covering acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Implementation section, last bullet ('Document the phase/floor manifest contract…')
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'docs/plan-review-gate.md exists and documents: the phase/floor manifest contract, the phase_status compatibility table, the fixed-0.80 floor rationale, the PlanReviewGeneration transaction protocol, and rollback behavior including the phase-precision loss window.' Add to Verification: 'Confirm docs/plan-review-gate.md contains all required sections.'

findings.finding: The Implementation says 'Claim, idempotent reuse, and drift refresh already share compute_validity(DEFAULT|DRIFT_REFRESH) and inherit phase enforcement.' Probing the codebase shows `drift_refresh_candidate` (attest.py:146-179) does NOT delegate to `compute_validity` — it has its own inline signature, manifest, regver, material, and deps checks. The 'already share' framing is false as a current-state claim; this sharing is a deliverable of prerequisite ticket 75de. A developer implementing this ticket, reading the plan, would believe the unified boundary already exists and skip the necessary prerequisite coordination check.
findings.location: Implementation bullet 5: 'Extend 75de's compute_validity plan-review branch ... Claim, idempotent reuse, and drift refresh already share compute_validity(DEFAULT|DRIFT_REFRESH) and inherit phase enforcement.'

findings.finding: AC 6 contradicts the rollback description: AC 6 asserts 'roll-forward restores the exact value,' but the Implementation's rollback section explicitly admits the phase field may be absent after old-code re-reduction and is only approximated by rebooting from compiled status, meaning the exact value is not guaranteed to be restored.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 6 vs Implementation last bullet (Rollback paragraph)
findings.suggested_fix: Split AC 6 into two parts: (a) 'SNAPSHOT compaction and non-rollback re-compaction preserve the field exactly' and (b) 'During a rollback window where old reducers re-reduce raw events, phase is rebooted from compiled status; the documentation acknowledges this precision loss.' Align the rollback docs accordingly.

findings.finding: The plan's only integration AC covers the signing transaction ('stable collection, under-lock HEAD re-verification, signature append, and commit end to end'). No AC exercises the full orchestration path where a ticket in execution phase causes `workflow_ops` to pass `execution_review=True` to `pass3_over_findings`, and the elevated 0.80 floor actually changes a blocking outcome. This is an automatic cutover — not an opt-in flag — so E6(f) applies: the new code path needs a criterion that exercises it end-to-end as it runs in production.
findings.location: Verification section; AC 'Execution Pass-3 destructures (block_threshold, blocking_enabled) and floors only the float threshold when blocking is enabled'; AC 'The production-path integration test covers stable collection, under-lock HEAD re-verification, signature append, and commit end to end'

findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for why `plan_review_phase` must live in BOTH the signed manifest (as `review-phase: planning|execution`) AND in compiled reducer state — the two placements serve different roles that are individually load-bearing but never explained together. Without this rationale an implementer may eliminate one placement, breaking the compatibility check: the manifest records the phase *at review time* (what the LLM actually reviewed under), while compiled state records the *current* phase; the compatibility table compares these two distinct values. Using only compiled state at claim time would compare current phase to current phase (a tautology), while using only a manifest-derived value at review time would require reading the prior attestation before generating a new one.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph beginning 'Restructure `build_manifest`'s fixed initial list' and paragraph beginning 'Add reducer-maintained `plan_review_phase`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the manifest paragraph, add: 'The signed `review-phase:` line records the phase the LLM review ran under; the reducer-compiled field records the current phase. These two are compared by `phase_status` at claim time to detect a planning→execution transition that occurred after the review was completed but before it was claimed — a transition that would make the planning attestation incompatible with the stricter execution floor.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that new full reviews write `review_phase` (and for execution, `priority_floor: 0.8` as a JSON number) to the sidecar in the exact v2 schema format, and add a corresponding unit test assertion.
evidence: 'new full reviews always write JSON review_phase as lowercase string planning or execution; planning omits priority_floor; execution writes JSON number priority_floor: 0.8'
No AC line reads: 'New full reviews write review_phase (and priority_floor for execution) to the sidecar JSON in the exact v2 schema format.'
The test file `test_plan_review_resign.py` is named for re-sign, not for the write path of new reviews.
findings.finding: The write side of the `plan_review_result_v2` schema — specifically that new full reviews always write `review_phase` (and `priority_floor` for execution) to the sidecar JSON — has no covering acceptance criterion. The AC covers only the *read/re-sign* path ('Re-sign parses the exact v2 JSON phase fields through `sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata`') but no criterion verifies that a new full review actually persists these fields.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Persist review-time phase data for failed-sign recovery'; AC section (no corresponding line)
scenarios: An implementer writes the re-sign reader but omits or mis-implements the writer (e.g., writes priority_floor as a string or omits review_phase). All re-sign tests pass because they use fixture sidecars; production re-sign fails silently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an expand-contract sequence for `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` and `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json`: introduce `review_phase` as optional (with a documented default) in a first step, validate all producers emit it, then promote to required — and cover this schema rollback path explicitly in the rollback section.
evidence: Plan states: 'add required review_phase: planning|execution to plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json and plan_review_decide_input.schema.json'
Rollback section covers reducer, snapshot, and raw-event re-reduction only — no mention of schema contract rollback or optional-first migration for these two JSON schemas.
Any existing workflow step or test that validates against these schemas without emitting review_phase would immediately fail on upgrade.
findings.finding: Adding `review_phase` as a **required** field to `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` and `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json` is a breaking schema contract change for any existing producer or consumer of those schemas. The plan's rollback section covers the reducer and snapshot layers but does not mention an expand-contract sequence (add optional → validate → promote to required) or a rollback path for these JSON schema changes specifically.
findings.location: Implementation – 'Thread phase through the production workflow' paragraph; AC line 'add required `review_phase` to `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` and `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json`'
scenarios: A CI pipeline validates precheck output against the schema before the workflow code is deployed — the required field breaks validation immediately.
A rollback of the feature leaves the schema with a required field but the precheck code no longer emitting it, causing hard failures.
A parallel branch or integration consumer reads plan_review_decide_input.schema.json and fails strict validation on legacy payloads.
findings.suggested_fix: In a first deploy: add `review_phase` as an optional field with a default of `planning` in both schemas and have the precheck always emit it. In a second deploy (after confirming all producers emit it): promote to required. Add a note to the Rollback section: 'On rollback, demote review_phase back to optional in both schemas before reverting the precheck code.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Emit a distinct structured WARN log event (e.g. `plan_review_phase_rollback_reboot`) when the forward-upgrade bootstrap fires on a snapshot that previously had the field absent due to a rollback re-reduction, distinguishing it from the normal pre-feature bootstrap, so operators can identify affected tickets.
evidence: 'If old code fully re-reduces raw events after its own cache bump, the phase field is absent and a later forward upgrade reboots it from compiled status; operators accept that documented loss of historical phase precision during the rollback window.'
No structured log event is specified for the rollback-induced bootstrap triggered by the forward upgrade after rollback
Bootstrap emits `plan_review_phase_bootstrap` for pre-feature snapshots but the plan does not distinguish or alert on the rollback-induced case
findings.finding: The plan's rollback scenario produces a silent phase-precision loss in production: when old code fully re-reduces raw events after its own cache bump, the `plan_review_phase` field is dropped and a later forward upgrade reboots from compiled status — but no structured log event is specified for this degradation path. The plan only documents it in `docs/plan-review-gate.md` and the rollback AC; operators have no runtime observable signal that phase data was reset.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Rollback disables claim/close enforcement first; old reducers preserve the unknown snapshot field...'
scenarios: An operator rolls back and then re-deploys the new version; the phase field silently resets to a status-inferred value for all tickets that were re-reduced during the rollback window. The operator has no way to know which tickets were affected without manually inspecting sidecar/manifest data.
findings.suggested_fix: In the bootstrap logic, add a flag or heuristic to detect when the snapshot was written by a version that should have had the field but doesn't (e.g., snapshot `_reducer_version` >= the feature's version but field absent), and emit a separate structured WARN rather than the normal INFO.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the plan explicitly accounts for the `additionalProperties: false` constraint in `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` and `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json`, and that all existing tests asserting against these schemas are updated.
evidence: plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json line 17: '"additionalProperties": false'
plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json line 16: required array lists all current fields without review_phase
Plan says to add review_phase as required — making it required in a closed schema is a hard breaking change
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'plan_review_precheck emits the compiled value' of review_phase, implying plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json will carry a required review_phase field. The existing schema has `additionalProperties: false` — which means this is a hard breaking-contract change: any cached or older workflow runner that validates against the old schema will reject the updated precheck output. The plan does not acknowledge this constraint or specify how in-flight workflow executions or cached schema validators are handled during rollout.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Thread phase through the production workflow: add required review_phase to plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json'
scenarios: A rollback that reverts the schema but leaves upgraded workflow runners causes schema validation failures
Any test that validates precheck output against the old schema (e.g. test_gate_engine_cutover.py) breaks without being updated

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
